name: Spell Slots
description: Regardless of how many spells a caster knows or prepares, they can cast only a limited number of spells before resting. Manipulating the fabric of magic and channeling its energy into even a simple spell is physically and mentally taxing. Each spell has a level from 0 to 9. Spell slots represent a caster's capacity to cast spells of 1st level and higher.
effects: To cast a spell of 1st level or higher, you must expend a slot of the spell's level or higher
You regain all expended spell slots when you finish a Long Rest
Some classes (Warlock) regain spell slots on a Short Rest
Cantrips (level 0 spells) can be cast at will without expending a spell slot
Cantrips scale in power as you gain levels (typically at levels 5, 11, and 17)

name: Casting a Spell at a Higher Level
description: When a spellcaster casts a spell using a slot that is of a higher level than the spell, the spell assumes the higher level for that casting. Many spells have more powerful effects when cast at a higher level.
effects: Some spells deal additional damage or have enhanced effects when cast with a higher-level slot
The spell description specifies what happens when cast at higher levels
The spell is considered to be the level of the slot used to cast it
Counterspell and Dispel Magic check against the level of the slot used, not the spell's base level

name: Spell Components
description: A spell's components are the physical requirements you must meet in order to cast it. Each spell's description indicates whether it requires Verbal (V), Somatic (S), or Material (M) components.
effects: Verbal (V): The caster must speak words of power; being unable to speak prevents casting
Somatic (S): The caster must gesture with at least one free hand; being restrained may prevent this
Material (M): The caster must have the specified materials in hand or a spellcasting focus
A spellcasting focus (arcane focus, druidic focus, holy symbol) can replace material components that have no cost listed
Material components with a specified gold cost must be provided and are not replaced by a focus
Components that are consumed by the spell must be provided each time you cast it

name: Concentration
description: Some spells require you to maintain Concentration in order to keep their magic active. If you lose Concentration, such a spell ends. You can end Concentration at any time (no action required).
effects: You can concentrate on only one spell at a time; casting a new Concentration spell ends the current one
Taking damage requires a Constitution saving throw to maintain Concentration (DC = 10 or half the damage taken, whichever is higher)
Being Incapacitated or killed ends Concentration immediately
The DM might decide environmental phenomena (a wave crashing over you) require a DC 10 Constitution saving throw
Normal activity such as moving and attacking doesn't break Concentration
You can end Concentration at any time on your turn (no action required)

name: Ritual Casting
description: Certain spells have a special tag: ritual. Such a spell can be cast following the normal rules for spellcasting, or it can be cast as a ritual. The ritual version of a spell takes 10 minutes longer to cast than normal and doesn't expend a spell slot.
effects: Casting a spell as a ritual takes 10 minutes longer than its normal casting time
Ritual casting does not expend a spell slot
The caster must have the spell prepared or on their spell list (depending on class features)
Wizards can cast ritual spells from their spellbook without having them prepared
The spell must have the Ritual tag to be cast as a ritual
The spell is cast at its base level when cast as a ritual (no upcasting)

name: Counterspell and Dispel Magic
description: Counterspell and Dispel Magic are key spells for interacting with other spellcasters. Counterspell interrupts a spell being cast, while Dispel Magic ends an ongoing spell effect.
effects: Counterspell: Reaction when you see a creature within 60 feet casting a spell
If the target spell is 3rd level or lower, it automatically fails (when Counterspell is cast at 3rd level)
If the target spell is 4th level or higher, make a spellcasting ability check (DC = 10 + spell's level) to counter it
Casting Counterspell at a higher level automatically counters spells of that level or lower
Dispel Magic: Automatically ends spells of 3rd level or lower on a target
For spells of 4th level or higher, make a spellcasting ability check (DC = 10 + spell's level)
Casting Dispel Magic at a higher level automatically dispels spells of that level or lower

name: Spell Attack Rolls
description: Some spells require the caster to make an attack roll to determine whether the spell effect hits the intended target. Your attack bonus with a spell attack equals your spellcasting ability modifier + your Proficiency Bonus.
effects: Spell attack modifier = spellcasting ability modifier + Proficiency Bonus
Melee spell attacks are made against targets within the spell's reach (usually touch)
Ranged spell attacks are made against targets within the spell's range
Ranged spell attacks have Disadvantage if a hostile creature is within 5 feet of you
A natural 20 is a critical hit; a natural 1 always misses

name: Spell Save DC
description: Many spells specify that a target can make a saving throw to avoid some or all of the spell's effects. The spell specifies the ability that the target uses for the save and what happens on a success or failure.
effects: Spell Save DC = 8 + spellcasting ability modifier + Proficiency Bonus
The spell description specifies which saving throw the target makes
On a successful save, the target typically takes half damage or avoids the effect entirely
Legendary Resistance allows some creatures to choose to succeed on a failed save (limited uses)

name: Areas of Effect
description: Spells such as Burning Hands and Cone of Cold cover an area, allowing them to affect multiple creatures at once. A spell's description specifies its area of effect.
effects: Cone: Extends from the point of origin in a direction the caster chooses; width at any point equals the distance from the point of origin
Cube: The point of origin is on one face of the cube; the size is the length of each side
Cylinder: The point of origin is the center of a circle of a particular radius; the cylinder extends from that circle to a certain height
Line: Extends from its point of origin in a straight path up to its length and covers an area defined by its width
Sphere: Extends outward from its point of origin; the size is the radius of the sphere
A spell's effect expands in straight lines from the point of origin; if no unblocked straight line extends from the point of origin, a creature is not affected

name: Combining Magical Effects
description: The effects of different spells add together while the durations of those spells overlap. The effects of the same spell cast multiple times don't combine, however.
effects: Different spells can stack their effects (e.g., Bless and Shield of Faith both apply)
The same spell from multiple sources does not stack; only the most potent version applies
The most potent effect applies while durations overlap (e.g., two castings of Bless - only one applies)
This rule applies to spells specifically; class features and other effects may stack differently

name: Prepared and Known Spells
description: Before a spellcaster can use a spell, they must have the spell firmly fixed in mind or must have access to the spell in a magic item. Different classes have different rules for how they prepare and learn spells.
effects: Prepared casters (Cleric, Druid, Paladin, Wizard) choose spells from their list each day after a Long Rest
Known casters (Bard, Ranger, Sorcerer, Warlock) know a fixed set of spells and swap one when they level up
The number of spells you can prepare or know depends on your class level and spellcasting ability modifier
You always have your cantrips available regardless of preparation
Some subclass features grant spells that are always prepared and don't count against your limit
